design_philosophy.core_loop: Party-based turn-based combat with ATB (Active Time Battle) system; explore field maps and dungeons, engage random encounters, advance narrative through story-driven character progression
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (HP/MP) in random encounters; narrative stakes through mandatory party composition changes that force tactical adaptation
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a diverse party of heroes through an epic world-spanning quest to stop an evil sorcerer; witness dramatic character arcs and relationships evolving through story
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneered dramatic storytelling in console RPGs by giving each character a fixed, unchangeable class tied to their narrative role; party composition shifts mid-game enforce playstyle adaptation; ATB system creates urgency in turn-based combat; emphatic narrative focus over mechanical complexity

difficulty.continues_system: Game Over restores from last save file; no explicit continues system
difficulty.checkpointing: Manual save points at towns and specific story locations; no mid-dungeon checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Japan received 'Easy Type' version released October 1991 (same year), indicating developer acknowledgment of difficulty concerns; no in-game difficulty selector documented for standard version

progression.upgrade_system: Equipment purchased in towns or found in dungeons; character stats grow through battle experience; spells/abilities tied to fixed character classes
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-driven character recruitment and dismissal; new areas unlock as narrative progresses; certain abilities tied to character class and story progression
replayability_hooks: Multiple versions with varying difficulty (Easy Type vs. standard)
Community ROM hacks created for HardType, Insane, and Solo Cecil Edition variants indicating desire for challenge scaling
Character class restrictions create strategic variety in party composition across playthroughs

world_building_techniques: Dramatic storytelling through mandatory character recruitment and dismissal arcs
Field map exploration revealing story context
NPC dialogue in towns providing lore and quest direction
Character-specific story moments tied to class evolution and vocation changes mid-game

boss_design.philosophy: Story-tied encounters with named antagonists (Golbez as primary sorcerer antagonist); boss encounters enforce narrative pacing and party adaptation

faction_indicators: Golbez (sorcerer antagonist seeking to seize crystals)
danger_signals: Crystal seizing plot indicates world-ending threat
Evil sorcerer antagonist represents existential danger
environmental_storytelling: Towns serve as safe havens for resource gathering
Towers, caves, and forests as thematic dungeon locations
Overworld travel reveals geography and world scope

sources_used: wikipedia - Final Fantasy IV gameplay mechanics, narrative structure, character systems, development credits
data_crystal - Technical specifications, regional variants (Easy Type), ROM information
